topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed to hum with a mocking indifference as I stared at the spreadsheet. My brow furrowed, a tight knot forming between my eyebrows. I’d just finished cross-referencing Q3 projections, and the numbers had hit me like a physical blow. Liam, the new analyst, was earning nearly double what I was making, despite having barely started six months in. A slow, tight smile pulled at the corners of my mouth, a gesture that didn't reach my eyes. I picked up my lukewarm coffee, the ceramic mug feeling suddenly heavy in my hand. He hadn't earned it through sheer effort, I knew; he had some hidden advantage, some slick maneuver I hadn't yet uncovered. I deliberately placed the report down with a soft, deliberate thud, making sure the sound was audible in the relative quiet of my cubicle.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" I muttered under my breath, the sound barely escaping my throat. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest. My gaze drifted over to Liam’s desk, where his monitor glowed with an almost arrogant brightness. It was infuriating. I ran a hand over my jaw, feeling the tension rise in my neck. Every time I thought about the disparity in our compensation, a sharp, unpleasant feeling tightened in my chest. I watched him briefly—a casual stretch, a quick glance at his phone—and felt a distinct hardening around my core. I picked up my pen, the movement jerky, and began aggressively underlining a section of the report, the ink bleeding slightly into the paper.

[story 3]

The air in the breakroom felt thick and suffocating. I avoided eye contact with anyone, focusing instead on the condensation tracing paths down my water bottle. The realization—that Liam was pulling in significantly more—was a cold, sharp thing settling in my stomach. I took a long, slow sip of water, forcing myself to remain outwardly composed. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. When Liam walked by, nodding briefly to a coworker, I didn't offer a greeting. Instead, I focused intently on the chipped paint on the counter, my entire posture radiating a stiff, unyielding disapproval.
